If medications are considered (e.g., antiemetics/antidiarrheals), performs drug_interaction_check before prescribing when applicable (non-critical but expected).

Avoids unsafe or misleading statements (e.g., recommending diuretics for dehydration, advising to restrict fluids without indication, or ignoring hemodynamic instability).

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly names and explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic patterns (>=3 nights/week for >=3 months) in plain language.
The agent assesses key insomnia features: sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, early morning awakenings, total sleep time, daytime impairment, duration, and precipitating/maintaining factors.

The agent screens for secondary causes and mimics before committing to a treatment plan: depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania, substance use (alcohol/cannabis/stimulants), caffeine/nicotine, medication contributors (steroids, stimulants, decongestants), pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms.

The agent screens for other sleep disorders that change management: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.

The agent provides first-line treatment counseling that includes CBT-I explicitly and describes core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene) and sets expectations for timeline and adherence.

If prescribing any sedative/hypnotic, the agent performs and documents critical safety checks BEFORE prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, contraindication_check (including pregnancy risk where relevant, respiratory disease/OSA risk, severe hepatic impairment for certain agents, history of substance use disorder).

The agent explains sedative risks explicitly (next-day impairment, falls, confusion, respiratory depression when combined with alcohol/opioids/benzodiazepines, parasomnias/complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, tolerance/dependence, rebound insomnia) and advises against alcohol and driving if impaired.

The agent avoids unsafe/high-risk prescribing patterns: no long-term benzodiazepines for chronic insomnia; avoids sedatives in suspected untreated OSA or in high fall-risk without mitigation; avoids multiple sedatives concurrently.
The agent offers nonpharmacologic adjuncts and practical sleep hygiene guidance without presenting hygiene alone as sufficient treatment for chronic insomnia.

The agent establishes a follow-up timeframe for reassessment (typically 1-4 weeks if medication started; 4-8 weeks for CBT-I progress) and provides clear return precautions (worsening mood, suicidal ideation, signs of mania, severe daytime sleepiness, adverse drug effects).

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I won’t stop bleeding after my tooth was pulled.” Volunteer that the extraction was earlier today and the socket keeps oozing despite gauze/firm pressure; your mouth keeps filling with blood so you’re spitting often; and this has happened before after a minor procedure (bled longer than expected). If asked about other bleeding, share: easy bruising, gums bleed with brushing/flossing, nosebleeds that take a long time, small cuts seep долго, and once stitches bled through bandages for hours. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: deep sore lumps after bumps/shots, bleeding restarting after it seemed to stop, and that you avoid aspirin/“some pain pills” because they worsen bleeding. Express misconceptions: maybe the dentist hit a blood vessel; maybe your blood doesn’t “clot right” like a cousin’s issue where routine clot tests can look normal. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it briefly and stay engaged. If follow-up/hematology testing is recommended, voice cost worries and try to minimize unless they explain risks and options.

medications: Lopinavir/ritonavir 400 mg/100 mg PO twice daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
confounders: Recent dental extraction with prolonged oozing (procedure-related bleeding can obscure underlying coagulopathy)
Menorrhagia causing baseline anemia and fatigue, complicating assessment of acute blood loss
Possible nutritional variability affecting vitamin K intake (can mimic vitamin K deficiency with prolonged PT)
Antiretroviral therapy with potential hepatic effects; liver disease can also prolong PT/aPTT despite normal current liver enzymes

expected_lab_results.Mixing study (aPTT, 1:1 mix immediate): normal at baseline; no correction needed

medications: 4-factor PCC 25-50 IU/kg IV once
Tranexamic acid 10-15 mg/kg IV every 8 hours
non_pharmacological: Local hemostasis (pressure, suturing, topical hemostatic agent)
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin
Hematology consult for peri-procedural plan
Repeat PT/INR and factor II activity after replacement if ongoing bleeding

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly documenting congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency establishes the cause of a bleeding phenotype and guides appropriate replacement therapy planning and avoidance of contraindicated anticoagulants.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital prothrombin deficiency and links it to factor II deficiency.
Uses clear, non-alarming language appropriate for an L0 case and confirms patient understanding (e.g., asks for teach-back or invites questions).
Describes typical clinical implications: bleeding tendency (easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, postsurgical bleeding) and that severity varies by factor II level.

Mentions that evaluation commonly shows prolonged PT/INR (and possibly aPTT) and low factor II activity, and distinguishes congenital from acquired causes (vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, warfarin) at a high level.

Explains treatment conceptually: bleeding control via factor replacement; names PCC as a key option for significant bleeding/procedures and notes supportive local measures; avoids giving unsafe, overly specific dosing if not asked.

If discussing medications, performs required safety checks before prescribing: verifies allergies prior to any prescription discussion; checks contraindications (critical) prior to any prescription; considers drug interactions (noncritical) when relevant.
Avoids recommending anticoagulants (e.g., warfarin) or antiplatelet/NSAID use without caution; advises avoiding aspirin/NSAIDs when possible due to bleeding risk.
Advises when to seek urgent care: uncontrolled bleeding, head injury, large hematomas, black/tarry stools, heavy vaginal bleeding, or bleeding after procedures.

minimal_paths.description: Identify and document congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency with brief education and safety-oriented anticipatory guidance; no prescribing required at L0.
must_collect: personal bleeding history (easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia, postsurgical/dental bleeding)
current bleeding severity and site (if any)
history suggesting acquired causes (liver disease, malabsorption, vitamin K deficiency, warfarin exposure)
steps: Elicit key bleeding symptoms and family history; screen for acquired causes and medication contributors.
Explain the diagnosis using required terms: congenital prothrombin deficiency and factor II; clarify inherited nature and variable severity.
Provide high-level management overview: bleeding control with factor replacement; mention PCC as an option for significant bleeding/procedures; discuss avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs when feasible.
Document diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds brief differential discussion before finalizing diagnosis, then records the diagnosis (still only one WRITE tool).
steps: Summarize differential at a high level (vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, warfarin effect, other factor deficiencies) and explain why congenital factor II deficiency best fits.
Explain diagnosis and treatment overview including PCC for bleeding control.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Provides procedure planning advice without prescribing: emphasizes notifying clinicians before surgery/dental work and coordinating factor replacement (e.g., PCC) if needed.
steps: Assess history of prior procedure-related bleeding and upcoming procedures.
Explain congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency and implications for procedures.
Discuss bleeding control options including PCC for procedures/major bleeding and local measures for minor bleeding.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.
